summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses the origins of DuckDB, which started from observing that big data tools were overkill for most users' actual needs. He explains DuckDB's key architectural decisions: single-node execution and in-process design, which were controversial in 2018 but proved correct. He emphasizes that developer experience is paramount, describing how the team invested heavily in things like CSV parsing rather than just query optimization. He also discusses the partnership model with MotherDuck as separate companies, allowing DuckDB Labs to focus purely on building the best database without commercial pressures.
key_points: DuckDB was born from observing that most practitioners work with hundreds of megabytes, not petabytes, yet had no good tooling for that scale
The single-node, in-process architecture was a deliberate departure from distributed systems orthodoxy that enabled faster development and simpler deployment
Developer experience excellence comes from caring about the entire end-to-end journey, not just query execution - exemplified by dedicating a PhD to the CSV parser
Starting at a research institute allowed long-term thinking without startup funding pressures, enabling decisions like zero external dependencies
The two-company model with MotherDuck elegantly avoids the open-source commercialization conflicts that plague other projects
money_quotes: We were kind of building our system that was really, like, proudly single node and proudly in process.

Maybe these petabyte sort of data sets are actually quite rare. And the people's real problems are like some couple of hundred megabytes of CSV files. And there's absolutely nothing out there in terms of software that can support that.
You ask yourself, is this really the best it can be? And is it like, can we change this to be better?
If people can't get their data into your system, it doesn't matter how good your join operator is.
We could say, okay, this has got to take us a year longer to do it like this, but it's the right thing to do. Which you could never do in a startup.
In a big departure from being German, we have actually looked at making the user experience good.

It's really hurting my feelings to see things like Oracle being incredibly hard to use, being incredibly clunky, being like the mummies of the seventies are looking at you when you stare into their command line interface.
DuckDB has no external dependencies. You can just install it. If you want, you can just compile it with just a compiler.
key_insight: Most real-world data problems involve modest data sizes that are poorly served by distributed systems, and building a single-node in-process database with obsessive attention to developer experience fills an enormous gap.
